False faces in clouds.<[silence:500]> Wiring for survival.<[silence:500]>

Pareidolia—the brain’s habit of spotting faces where none exist—comes from Latin par (faulty) + eidōlon (image).<[silence:500]> It’s a misfire of the same system that lets you recognize a friend in a crowd.<[silence:500]>

Your brain treats faces like emergency alerts.<[silence:500]> A cluster of neurons called the fusiform face area fires 300 milliseconds after seeing two dots over a line (eyes + mouth), even on a toaster.<[silence:500]> This shortcut evolved when missing a predator’s glare meant death.<[silence:500]> Now, it turns power outlets into scowls and tree bark into ghosts.<[silence:500]>

Infants as young as 4 days old prefer face-like patterns over random shapes, proving the bias is hardwired.<[silence:500]> In 2009, researchers showed adults blurred images:<[silence:400]> 92% "saw" faces in noise when primed with fear.<[silence:500]> Even macaques—monkeys with no cartoon exposure—react to three-stroke "faces" drawn in dirt.<[silence:500]>

Three related subjects are fear’s neural shortcuts, how babies decode faces, and why AI hallucinates patterns.<[silence:500]>
clean_story:
False faces in clouds. Wiring for survival.

Pareidolia—the brain’s habit of spotting faces where none exist—comes from Latin par (faulty) + eidōlon (image). It’s a misfire of the same system that lets you recognize a friend in a crowd.

Your brain treats faces like emergency alerts. A cluster of neurons called the fusiform face area fires 300 milliseconds after seeing two dots over a line (eyes + mouth), even on a toaster. This shortcut evolved when missing a predator’s glare meant death. Now, it turns power outlets into scowls and tree bark into ghosts.

Infants as young as 4 days old prefer face-like patterns over random shapes, proving the bias is hardwired. In 2009, researchers showed adults blurred images: 92% "saw" faces in noise when primed with fear. Even macaques—monkeys with no cartoon exposure—react to three-stroke "faces" drawn in dirt.

Three related subjects are fear’s neural shortcuts, how babies decode faces, and why AI hallucinates patterns.
